examples.title: Basic GROUP BY with Aggregate
examples.description: Finding count and average salary per department
examples.code:
SELECT 
    department,
    COUNT(*) AS employee_count,
    AVG(salary) AS avg_salary,
    SUM(salary) AS total_salary,
    MIN(salary) AS min_salary,
    MAX(salary) AS max_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
examples.explanation: Groups employees by department and calculates various aggregates for each group. COUNT(*) counts all employees, AVG calculates average salary.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting for GROUP BY
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of groups

examples.title: GROUP BY with HAVING
examples.code:
SELECT 
    department,
    AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;
examples.explanation: First groups by department, calculates average, then filters groups where avg_salary > 50000, finally sorts by average in descending order.

examples.title: Multiple Column GROUP BY
examples.description: Grouping by department and job title
examples.code:
SELECT 
    department,
    job_title,
    COUNT(*) AS emp_count,
    AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
WHERE hire_date >= '2020-01-01'
GROUP BY department, job_title
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
ORDER BY department, avg_salary DESC;
examples.explanation: Groups by unique department-job_title combinations. WHERE filters before grouping, HAVING filters after. Shows only groups with 2+ employees.

examples.title: Equivalent Pandas GroupBy
examples.description: Python/Pandas equivalent of SQL GROUP BY and HAVING
examples.code:
import pandas as pd

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('employees.csv')

# Equivalent to: SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department
grouped = df.groupby('department').size()

# Equivalent to: SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000
result = df.groupby('department')['salary'].mean()
result = result[result > 50000]

# Multiple columns equivalent
multi_group = df.groupby(['department', 'job_title']).agg({
    'salary': ['count', 'mean', 'sum'],
    'employee_id': 'count'
}).reset_index()
examples.explanation: Pandas groupby() provides similar functionality to SQL GROUP BY. The filter (HAVING equivalent) is applied after aggregation using boolean indexing.

examples.title: Advanced: Using HAVING with Multiple Conditions
examples.description: Complex filtering with multiple aggregate conditions
examples.code:
SELECT 
    category,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    AVG(price) AS avg_price,
    SUM(stock) AS total_stock
FROM products
WHERE discontinued = 0
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 
   AND AVG(price) BETWEEN 100 AND 1000
   AND SUM(stock) > 1000
ORDER BY total_stock DESC;
examples.explanation: Multiple HAVING conditions combined with AND. All conditions must be true for a group to be included in results. Uses BETWEEN for range filtering.
